- 6.5 The vaccinator ensures the adverse event, and any subsequent decisions relating to the event, are effectively communicated to the individual/parent/guardian and clearly documented in the child's *Well Child Tamariki Ora My Health Book* (if applicable) and in the patient records, and appropriate follow-up is carried out.

# **A3.4 Guidelines for organisations storing vaccines and/or offering immunisation services**

These guidelines apply to all organisations that store vaccines and/or offer immunisation services, including (but not limited to) general practices, public health units, community pharmacies, travel clinics, occupational health clinics, emergency medical services, research units and hospital wards/clinics/departments/pharmacies.

# The organisation that employs vaccinators to offer immunisation services has links to primary health care and to Well Child Tamariki Ora providers

#### Required characteristics

Immunisation is not delivered in isolation, but as an integrated part of primary health care services, including Well Child Tamariki Ora for children.

If possible, at the time of immunisation, the organisation undertakes other health promotion and/or disease prevention activities as applicable, such as the Well Child National Schedule or Care Plus.

Immunisation events, childhood and adult, are well communicated to other health services linked to the individual (eg, primary health care, outreach immunisation services, pharmacies, occupational health).

## The organisation achieves high immunisation coverage of its population

#### Required characteristics

The organisation has an effective, secure, NHI-based system for recording and reporting immunisations and identifying individuals requiring immunisation.

Respecting the individual's/parent's/guardian's rights to make an informed choice, the organisation takes all steps to ensure that an individual's immunisation schedule commences on time and that subsequent events are administered on the due date.

The organisation has electronic linkage to the AIR for registration and immunisation event notification, and the AIR is used to assist with follow-up. If electronic linking is not available, manual processes must be used.

The organisation has a robust reminder (pre-call) system which encourages the delivery of on-time immunisation and timely follow-up for overdue immunisation.

The organisation has an effective communication strategy to target high-needs population groups.

Attendance at the practice/organisation is used as an opportunity to remind individuals/parents/guardians of the importance of immunisation and, if appropriate, to check and offer to bring up to date the individual's immunisation status.

Those who do not respond to recall and who have not declined to take part are referred to the outreach immunisation service, as per local protocol.

# The organisation supports vaccinators and AIR administrators Required characteristics

The organisation has comprehensive immunisation-related policies based on best practice, informed consent, the vaccination process and management of adverse events.

The organisation uses a pharmaceutical refrigerator to store vaccines, has a vaccine cold chain policy in place and meets the requirements outlined in the *National Standard for Vaccine Storage and Transportation for Immunisation Providers 2017 (2nd edition[\)](#page-638-0)*<sup>3</sup> for all areas within the organisation storing vaccines.

The organisation provides training and support workers (eg, kaiāwhina, community health workers) for vaccinators working in the community.

The organisation supports the need for vaccinators to have access to ongoing education and training on all aspects of immunisation at least every two years and when there are changes to the Schedule.

The organisation provides initial and ongoing training and support specific to the AIR, PMS, and/or the SBVS (if applicable).

# The service is readily available, with no barriers to access Required characteristics

No fee is charged to the individual/guardian for the immunisations that are on the Schedule or high-risk programmes (or for completing the child's immunisation certificate). Regardless of their immigration and citizenship status, all children aged

<span id="page-638-0"></span><sup>3</sup> Refer to the National Immunisation Programme cold chain management (available at **[www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/health-services-and-programmes/vaccine-information/vaccine-service](http://www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/health-services-and-programmes/vaccine-information/vaccine-service-delivery/cold-chain-standards-for-vaccines)[delivery/cold-chain-standards-for-vaccines](http://www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/health-services-and-programmes/vaccine-information/vaccine-service-delivery/cold-chain-standards-for-vaccines)**).

under 18 years are eligible to receive funded Schedule vaccines, and providers may claim the immunisation benefit for these children. Non-residents who were under age 18 years when they commenced HPV vaccination in New Zealand are currently funded to complete the course, even if they are aged 18 years or older when they complete it. Further information on eligibility can be found on the Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora website (**[www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/for-health-providers/publicly-funded](https://www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/for-health-providers/publicly-funded-health-and-disability-services/)[health-and-disability-services/](https://www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/for-health-providers/publicly-funded-health-and-disability-services/)**).

Immunisations are provided to both enrolled and casual patients, at all times when the organisation or service is open.

A person's immunisation status is checked at each visit to the service.

The organisation is culturally appropriate (ie, all health workers are assessed as culturally competent, reflect the populations they serve and offer a range of health information resources[4](#page-639-0) in different languages).

# **Appendix 4: Authorisation and criteria of vaccinators**

This section provides an overview of vaccinator requirements and authorisation. There are various types of vaccinators in Aotearoa, including those who are approved by Medical Officers of Health and others who are enabled to vaccinate through an extension to their scope of practice.

# **A4.1 Regulations and pathways that enable authorisation**

### A4.1.1 Medicines Regulations

All vaccines are prescription medicines. The Medicines Regulations 1984 specify requirements that allow vaccinators to be authorised to administer vaccines and administer and/or supply medicines without a prescription for the purpose of an approved immunisation programme. Approved medicines for use in approved immunisation programmes are detailed in Appendix 9.

## <span id="page-641-0"></span>A4.1.2 Authority for approval of authorised vaccinators

A person may be authorised under Regulation 44A(2) of the Medicines Regulations 1984 by the Director-General of Health or a Medical Officer of Health to administer a vaccine and administer and/or supply medicines for the purposes of an approved immunisation programme.

Any registered health professional whose scope of practice allows for vaccination, is eligible to complete the training and authorisation pathway and become an authorised vaccinator.

Successful applicants will be authorised to administer either all scheduled vaccines or specific vaccines depending on the training and clinical assessment they have completed, as detailed in section [A4.5.](#page-650-0)

Successful applicants will also be authorised to administer and/or supply medicines approved for use in approved immunisation programmes, as detailed in Appendix 9.

Regulation 44A(2) stipulates that the person seeking approval must apply in writing to the Director-General or a Medical Officer of Health, and provide documentary evidence that they:

1. can carry out basic emergency techniques including resuscitation and the treatment of anaphylaxis

- 2. have knowledge of the safe and effective handling of immunisation products and equipment
- 3. can demonstrate clinical interpersonal skills
- 4. have knowledge of the relevant diseases and vaccines to be able to explain the vaccination to the individual, parent or guardian who is to consent to the vaccination on behalf of the individual, to ensure that the individual or parent or guardian of the individual can give informed consent to the vaccination.

## A4.1.3 Authority for approval of vaccinating health workers

In May 2022, a pathway to create a new supervised vaccinator role - a vaccinating health worker (VHW), was enabled through an amendment to the Medicines Regulations 1984, 44AA Alternate authorisation of vaccinators. The regulations specify that the authorised person must, at all times while performing the tasks authorised under Regulation 44AA, work under the clinical supervision and direction of a suitably qualified health practitioner.

Regulation 44AA(3) stipulates that the person must apply to the Director-General of Health or a National Medical Officer of Health and provide documentary evidence that they:

- 1. have successfully completed training as approved by the Director-General
- 2. can carry out basic emergency techniques, including resuscitation and the treatment of anaphylaxis and
- 3. have knowledge of the safe handling of immunisation products and equipment.

# A4.1.4 Authority for approval of pharmacist vaccinators and intern pharmacist vaccinators

Pharmacist and intern pharmacist vaccinators are required to work according to the Medicines Classifications which define specific conditions for each vaccine.

The Medicines Classification Committee has reclassified a number of vaccines to allow administration without a prescription by pharmacists or registered intern pharmacists who:

- 1. successfully complete the *Vaccinator Foundation Course (VFC)* or any equivalent training course approved by the Ministry of Health.
- 2. successfully complete a clinical assessment
- 3. comply with the immunisation standards of the Ministry of Health.

Pharmacist and intern pharmacist vaccinators are not required to apply for authorised vaccinator status to be able to administer vaccines that have been reclassified.

Pharmacist and intern pharmacist vaccinators are expected to be aware of which vaccines have been reclassified for their scope in Schedule 1 of the Medicines Regulations 1984 (see [Table A4.2\)](#page-646-0).

Pharmacist vaccinators can choose to become authorised vaccinators either by applying to their local Medical Officer of Health or through the national transition to authorised vaccinator pathway (see details in section [A4.1.2\)](#page-641-0). Additional restrictions may apply depending on clinical assessment and local commissioning arrangements. Intern pharmacist vaccinators are not eligible to become authorised vaccinators.

Pharmacist vaccinators and intern pharmacist vaccinators must complete an approved vaccinator update course every two years, and maintain current resuscitation qualifications, to be able to continue to administer vaccines.

To demonstrate current vaccinator status, pharmacist vaccinators and intern pharmacist vaccinators must be able to provide evidence of the following:

- 1. completed Vaccinator Foundation Course (VFC), and
- 2. clinical assessment, and
- 3. current resuscitation certification (see section [A4.1.2\)](#page-641-0) and
- 4. the two most recent vaccinator update courses, see section [A4.5](#page-650-0) for more information.

### A4.1.5 Previous approved vaccinator roles

- **Provisionally authorised vaccinators:** This role was established to support the COVID-19 Pandemic response. Authority for provisional vaccinators expired on 1 June 2023.
- **COVID-19 Vaccinators Working Under Supervision (CVWUS):** This supervised vaccinator role was established under regulation 44AB Authorisation of COVID-19 vaccinator, to support the COVID-19 Pandemic response. Authority for approved CVWUS expired on 1 June 2023 when Regulation 44AB was revoked. The CVWUS role was replaced with the Vaccinating Health Worker role.

# A4.1.6 Approved immunisation programmes

Authorised vaccinators are authorised to administer a **vaccine and administer and/or supply medicines** for the purposes of an approved immunisation programme.

An approved immunisation programme, as defined in the Medicines Regulations 1984, means a vaccination programme:

- pursuant to the National Immunisation Schedule administered by Pharmac; or
- approved by the Director-General or a Medical Officer of Health

Approved immunisation programmes are implemented as either National Immunisation Programmes or Local Immunisation Programmes. These are approved by the Director-General of Health or a Medical Officer of Health.

### A4.1.7 Medical Officers of Health

Medical Officers of Health are designated by the Director-General of Health under the Health Act 1956. They are medical practitioners suitably qualified and experienced in

public health medicine and are usually based within public health services in each district or region.

For the purpose of clarifying the Medical Officer of Health role in the vaccinator authorisation process.

- **Local Medical Officer of Health** refers to Medical Officers of Health authorising vaccinators in their district or region.
- **National Medical Officer of Health**, in the context of the vaccinator authorisation, refers to Medical Officers of Health authorising vaccinators across all districts via the national vaccinator authorisation portal.

# **A4.2 Vaccinator types**

There are five main vaccinator types as listed below.

### • **Authorised vaccinators**[1](#page-644-0)

- Includes age-restricted vaccinators and whole-of-life vaccinators.
- Includes extension pathways for example, BCG and Yellow Fever vaccinators.

#### • **Pharmacist (and intern pharmacist) vaccinators**

- Includes age-restricted and medicines classifications restrictions.
- Pharmacist vaccinators can upskill to whole-of-life authorised vaccinators.

#### • **Vaccinating health workers**

- Supervised role.
- Includes stage 1 and stage 2 vaccinating health workers.

#### • **Authorised prescribers with vaccines in their scope of practice**.

- The Medicines Act empowers authorised prescribers (including designated prescribers) to administer prescription medicines to people in their care.
- Authorised prescribers with vaccination within their scope of practice are responsible for understanding which if any vaccines they are authorised to administer, what populations they can administer to and any educational requirements to administer vaccines.
- Appropriately qualified authorised prescribers with vaccination in their scope of practice, for example, medical practitioners, nurse practitioners, and registered midwives, do not need further authorisation under Regulation 44A to carry out immunisations within their scope of practice.

#### • **Regulated health workers administering vaccines under prescription or standing orders issued by prescriber**.

- This includes for example registered nurses administering vaccines in a hospital ward or outpatient setting, or other clinic settings, pursuant to charted orders.
- This vaccinator group may or may not have completed an approved vaccinator training course. This group is strongly recommended to undertake vaccinator training to ensure that, at a minimum, they have knowledge of the safe and effective handling of immunisation products and equipment.

<span id="page-644-0"></span><sup>1</sup> Authorised vaccinators were previously called 'fully authorised vaccinators' and 'authorised independent vaccinators'.

# **A4.3 Vaccinator scopes of authorisation**

Restrictions may apply depending on scope of authorisation, vaccine reclassification and/or district funding to age groups that a vaccinator can administer vaccines to. It is the vaccinator's responsibility to understand and adhere to these restrictions.

All authorised vaccinators are responsible for working within their own scope of practice and ensuring their practice environment supports the provision of safe, quality immunisation programmes.

Vaccinating Health Workers cannot complete pre-vaccine screening or obtain informed consent from the consumer; this must be completed by a registered health practitioner. However, VHW can confirm with the consumer that informed consent has been obtained.

| only           | Tdap                                     | Ages 11 years and over | Deltoid only        |
|                | HPV                                      | Ages 11 years and over |                     |

a. Upon completion of appropriate education course.

b. Restrictions may apply depending on scope of authorisation, clinical assessment undertaken, vaccine reclassification and/or district funding to age groups that vaccinator can administer vaccines to. It is the vaccinator's responsibility to understand and adhere to these restrictions.

c. Vastus lateralis can be used when deltoid is not appropriate, and the vaccinator is trained and confident in the use of the site.

# <span id="page-648-0"></span>**A4.4 Roles and functions supporting the assessment and authorisation process**

#### **Clinical Assessors**

Clinical assessors are approved by a Local Medical Officer of Health to undertake authorised vaccinator, intern and pharmacist vaccinator clinical assessments.

They are typically experienced authorised vaccinators that have undertaken additional education and are often working as Immunisation Coordinators.

#### **Supervisors of Authorised Vaccinators**

An authorised vaccinator or pharmacist vaccinator who has at least six months' relevant experience appropriate to the age range being vaccinated.

#### **Practical Assessors of Vaccinating Health Workers**

Authorised vaccinators and pharmacist vaccinators with a minimum six months vaccinating experience.

#### **Supervisors of Vaccinating Health Workers**

Authorised vaccinator or pharmacist vaccinator who has recent and relevant vaccination experience. It is recommended they complete the free **[Supervisors of Vaccinating Health](https://www.immune.org.nz/catalogue/2022-supervisors-of-vaccinating-health-workers)  [Workers](https://www.immune.org.nz/catalogue/2022-supervisors-of-vaccinating-health-workers)** course.

#### **Medical Officers of Health & Public Health Services**

Vaccinator authorisations are approved by Medical Officers of Health; these roles are technically all National Medical Officers of Health. For the purpose of clarifying the vaccinator authorisation process for the Immunisation Handbook:

- Local Medical Officers of Health refers to Medical Officers of Health authorising vaccinators in their district.
- National Medical Officers of Health refers to Medical Officers of Health authorising vaccinators via the national vaccinator authorisation portal.

Public Health services maintain a register of the authorised vaccinators in their region.

#### **Vaccinator Authorisation Portal – [Workforce Requests Portal](https://www.workforcerequests.health.nz/)**

The portal is an online platform for new and existing vaccinators to apply for authorisation. It is being phased in with the expectation of managing all vaccinator authorisations over time. The portal is currently used to authorise:

- Vaccinating Health Workers
- pharmacist vaccinators transitioning to become authorised vaccinators
- age-restricted authorised vaccinators applying to vaccinate whole-of-life.

#### **Immunisation Advisory Centre (IMAC)**

IMAC is commissioned by Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora to provide clinical advice and education to health providers and vaccinators, and training to vaccinators across Aotearoa.

# **A4.6 Extending skills/ removing age restriction status**

Pharmacist vaccinators and age-restricted authorised vaccinators can undertake additional training and clinical assessment to be authorised as a whole-of-life authorised vaccinator.

<span id="page-652-2"></span>**Table A4.7: Process to extend vaccinator skills for whole-of-life authorisation**

|      | Pharmacist vaccinator or age-restricted authorised vaccinator                                                                                                                                          |  |
|------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--|
| Step |                                                                                                                                                                                                        |  |
| 1.   | Complete the 'Extending Vaccinator Skills (whole-of-life)' course, have a current APC and CPR<br>training certificate.                                                                                 |  |
|      | Apply for authorisation via the Health New Zealand   Te Whatu Ora Workforce Requests Portal<br>(see A4.7.1)                                                                                            |  |
|      | This will enable the vaccinator to:                                                                                                                                                                    |  |
| 2.   | Administer national schedule vaccines without the need for a standing order or prescription.<br>•                                                                                                      |  |
|      | Administer vaccines to people under 3 years old, under direct supervision, whilst gaining<br>•<br>experience and confidence before completing a clinical assessment for a person under 2 years<br>old. |  |
| 3.   | Complete supervised practice.                                                                                                                                                                          |  |
| 4.   | Complete a workbook.                                                                                                                                                                                   |  |
| 5.   | Successfully complete a clinical assessment on a person under 2 years old.                                                                                                                             |  |
| 6.   | Submit workbook and clinical assessment documentation to the Health New Zealand   Te Whatu<br>Ora Workforce Requests portal to be authorised as a whole-of-life authorised vaccinator.                 |  |

# <span id="page-652-0"></span>**A4.7 Applying for Authorisation**

# <span id="page-652-1"></span>A4.7.1 [Workforce Requests Portal](https://www.workforcerequests.health.nz/)

The Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora **[Workforce Requests Portal](https://www.workforcerequests.health.nz/)** is an online portal for vaccinators to apply for authorisation. This portal is currently used to authorise:

- vaccinating health workers
- pharmacist vaccinators transitioning to become authorised vaccinators
- age-restricted authorised vaccinators applying to vaccinate whole-of-life

Portal use will expand to include all authorised vaccinators and BCG endorsed vaccinators through a regional roll out beginning in late 2025. Below is a table of dates showing when vaccinators start using the portal in each region.

**Table A4.8: Start dates for vaccinators to use the Workforce Requests Portal to apply for authorisation and renewals**

| Region                          | Starting dates for vaccinators |
|---------------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Northern                        | 5 November 2025                |
| Midlands   Te<br>Manawa Taki    | 11 February 2026               |
| Central   Te Ikaroa             | 11 February 2026               |
| South Island   Te<br>Waipounamu | 5 November 2025                |

Until the portal is available in your region, your local authorisation requirements and settings will apply.

### A4.7.2 Authorised vaccinators

- Authorised vaccinators must apply for authorisation from a Medical Officer of Health providing evidence all requirements listed in [Table A4.7](#page-652-2) have been met.
- Authorisation forms for local authorisation processes are available on the Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora website **[Vaccinator authorisation process](https://www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/for-health-professionals/hospital-and-specialist-services/public-health/immunisation/vaccinator-authorisation-process#application-process-and-forms)**.
- Authorisation is valid once a letter of confirmation has been received.
- Authorisation letters may contain age restrictions or vaccination site restrictions, reflecting the clinical assessment ages.

**Table A4.9: Authorised vaccinator authorisation process using the [Workforce](https://www.workforcerequests.health.nz/)  [Requests Portal](https://www.workforcerequests.health.nz/)**

|      | Authorised vaccinators                                                                                                                                                                                                             |  |
|------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--|
| Step |                                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |  |
| 1.   | Complete the 'Vaccinator Foundation' course, have a current APC and CPR training certificate.                                                                                                                                      |  |
| 2.   | Apply for authorisation via the Workforce Requests Portal (see A4.7.1)<br>This will enable the vaccinator to:<br>Administer national schedule vaccines to all ages, without the need for a standing order or<br>•<br>prescription. |  |
| 3.   | Complete supervised practice.                                                                                                                                                                                                      |  |
| 4.   | Complete vaccinator skills log.                                                                                                                                                                                                    |  |
| 5.   | Successfully complete a clinical assessment.                                                                                                                                                                                       |  |
| 6.   | Submit vaccinator skills log and clinical assessment documentation via the Workforce Requests<br>Portal to be authorised as either:<br>Supervised for vaccinating people under three years old, or<br>•<br>Whole-of-life.<br>•     |  |

### A4.7.3 Pharmacist and intern pharmacist vaccinators

Pharmacist and intern pharmacist vaccinators are required to advise the local immunisation coordinator of their intention to set up a new pharmacist vaccination service. This is to ensure that local immunisation coordinator is aware of which pharmacies require three-yearly spatial logging of the refrigerator.

Pharmacist and intern pharmacist vaccinators are responsible for completing all steps to demonstrate successful completion of the education pathway and are required to provide evidence of this as part of Medicines Control audit processes. Intern pharmacists transition automatically to Pharmacist vaccinators after completing their intern year.

There is no requirement currently to inform the Pharmacy Society of New Zealand (PSNZ) about vaccination status.

Pharmacist vaccinators can apply for authorisation through their local authorisation process or through the national vaccinator authorisation portal. See [Table A4.7.](#page-652-2)

Pharmacies must obtain a contract from Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora to administer funded National Immunisation Schedule (NIS) vaccines and have them listed on their Integrated Community Pharmacy Services Agreement (ICPSA). Please note that not all NIS vaccines are eligible to be funded administration in a pharmacy setting (e.g. those listed as Xpharm by Pharmac).

### A4.7.4 Vaccinating Health Workers

Both VHW Stage 1 and 2 are authorised through the Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora **[Workforce Requests Portal](https://www.workforcerequests.health.nz/)**. Refer to [Table A4.10](#page-655-0) for the VHW Stage 1 and [Table](#page-656-0)  [A4.11](#page-656-0) for the Stage 2 authorisation process. Further detail is provided at **[Health New](https://www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/health-services-and-programmes/vaccine-information/vaccine-service-delivery/vaccinating-workforce/vaccinating-health-worker)  Zealand | Te Whatu Ora – [Vaccinating health worker](https://www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/health-services-and-programmes/vaccine-information/vaccine-service-delivery/vaccinating-workforce/vaccinating-health-worker)**.

#### <span id="page-655-0"></span>**Table A4.10: VHW Stage 1 authorisation process**

#### **VHW Stage 1** - VHW stage 1 has no pre-requisites.

#### Step

- 1. Complete the Vaccinating Health Worker Stage 1 course and Vaccinating Health Worker Stage 1 practical workshop.
- 2. Apply for authorisation to work under direct supervision. This means they can, under direct (oneto-one) supervision, vaccinate without the need for a standing order or prescription. Applicants need to provide the following evidence:
  - 'Vaccinating Health Worker Stage 1' course certificate
  - a current CPR certificate in basic life support (anaphylaxis management is covered through Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora-approved IMAC training for all vaccinators).
- 3. Once applicants have completed the workbook, and a practical assessment they submit these to the Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora [Workforce Requests Portal](https://www.workforcerequests.health.nz/) (se[e A4.7.1\)](#page-652-1)to be authorised to work under one-to-six supervision.

As the VHW Stage 1 progresses through the stages of authorisation, they will receive an authorisation letter that describes their scope and the supervision requirements.

VHWs, their supervisors and their employers should carefully review the authorisation letters to ensure the VHW is working within the scope of their authorisation.

---



#### **Table A4.11: VHW Stage 2 authorisation process**

**VHW Stage 2** - to enter the VHW 2 pathway, all applicants are required to have:

- VHW Stage 1 authorisation
- completed the Second Checker course
- completed at least 25 vaccination events over the previous 8 weeks

#### Step

- 1. After completion of the Vaccinating Health Worker Stage 2 course and Vaccinating Health Worker Stage 2 practical workshop, applicants apply through the portal for authorisation to work with supervision conditions for new age groups. This means you can, under direct (oneto-one) supervision, vaccinate younger age groups without the need for a standing order or prescription
- 2. Provide the following evidence:
  - 'Vaccinating Health Worker Stage 2' course certificate
  - a current CPR certificate in basic life support (anaphylaxis management is covered through Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora-approved IMAC training for all vaccinators).
- 3. Once you've completed the workbook, and a practical assessment you'll submit these to the portal to be authorised to work under one-to-six supervision.

As the VHW Stage 2 progresses through the stages of authorisation, they will receive an authorisation letter that describes the scope of the VHW and the supervision requirements.

VHWs, their supervisors and their employers should carefully review the authorisation letters to ensure the VHW is working within the scope of their authorisation. For example, after the Step 2 described above the VHW Stage 2 will be able to administer MMR to people aged 11 and over while they complete their workbook and practical assessment under supervision.

# **A4.8 Process when an authorised vaccinator is new to the health district in which they intend to practice**

All vaccinators authorised in the *Workforce requests portal* can practice nationally. Vaccinators authorised in districts are also approved to practice nationally until 1 June 2026. If an authorised vaccinator wishes to practise outside of their home area, they must advise the local Medical Officer of Health and Immunisation Coordinator of the new area and send through a copy of their current authorisation.

# **A4.9 Maintaining vaccinator authorisation and pharmacist vaccinator status**

#### A4.9.1 Continuing education

Authorised vaccinators and pharmacist vaccinators must complete an approved update course every 2 years.

- The first update must be completed within 2 years of attendance at the foundation course.
- Ongoing updates should be completed a minimum of 2 years since the date of the last update course.
- CPR certification that meets the requirements must be maintained as a requirement of authorisation (see section [A4.12](#page-661-0) for details).

#### A4.9.2 Renewal of authorised vaccinator authorisation

Authorised vaccinators need to apply for renewal of their authorisation to their local Medical Officer of Health, submitting all relevant documentation (i.e., immunisation update course certificate and a current cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR) certificate). See section [A4.12.](#page-661-0)

#### A4.9.3 Workforce Requests Portal

The [Workforce Requests Portal](https://www.workforcerequests.health.nz/) will be available for all renewals of authorised vaccinators and BCG endorsed vaccinators through a regional roll out beginning in late 2025. Below is a table of dates showing when vaccinators start using the portal in each region. Until the portal is available in your region, your local authorisation requirements and settings will apply. See [Table A4.8.](#page-653-0)

Applications for renewal should be submitted prior to the expiry of current authorisation. Vaccinators must stop vaccinating if their current authorisation expires before a new authorisation letter has been received.

### A4.9.4 Renewal of vaccinating health worker authorisation

A vaccinating health worker (VHW) is authorised to vaccinate within their scope for two years from the date of the update training certificate. To maintain status as an authorised VHW, authorisation must be renewed two yearly and the vaccinator must meet the requirements specified below:

- Complete a '*Vaccinating Health Worker* update' course approved by Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora during the past two years or within three months of expiry date on authorisation letter
- Provide evidence of a current CPR certificate (see section [A4.12](#page-661-0) for details).
- Submit this evidence to receive updated authorisation letter.

Applications for renewal should be submitted prior to the expiry of current authorisation. Vaccinators must stop vaccinating if their current authorisation expires before a new authorisation letter has been received.

#### A4.9.5 Maintaining pharmacist vaccinator status

Pharmacist vaccinators are required to maintain their own records to demonstrate continuing vaccinator education and CPR qualifications as listed in section [A4.12.](#page-661-0)

# **A4.10 Process when authorisation or pharmacist vaccinator status has not been maintained**

Any delay in maintaining vaccinator status means the vaccinator can no longer administer vaccines without standing orders or prescriptions until updated authorisation / pharmacist vaccinator status requirements have been met.

The approving Medical Officer of Health has discretion to reinstate an authorisation at any point, where the criteria under the Medicines Regulations are established.

#### A4.10.1 Lapsed authorised vaccinator or lapsed pharmacist vaccinator status

**Table A4.12: Actions required to have a vaccinator authorisation reinstated after specific lapsed periods for authorised vaccinators or pharmacist vaccinators.**

| Lapsed period      | Action                                                                                                     |  |
|--------------------|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--|
| Less than 90 days  | Complete an approved update course.                                                                        |  |
| 90 days to 2 years | Complete an approved update course.<br>Complete a full clinical assessment with an approved assessor.      |  |
| More than 2 years  | Complete a vaccinator foundation course.<br>Complete a full clinical assessment with an approved assessor. |  |

Vaccinators are required to provide evidence of a current practicing certificate and current CPR that meets requirements details at [A4.12.](#page-661-0)

- Authorised vaccinator status is not valid until an updated authorisation letter is received by the vaccinator.
- Pharmacist vaccinator status is valid once actions required are completed.

#### A4.10.2 Lapsed vaccinating health worker (VHW) authorisation

**Table A4.13: Actions required to have a vaccinator authorisation reinstated after specific lapsed periods for vaccinating health workers**

| Lapsed period                                                                                                 | Action                                                                                             |  |
|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--|
| Less than 90 days                                                                                             | Complete an approved update course.                                                                |  |
| 90 days to 2 years                                                                                            | Complete an approved VHW Stage 1 or Stage 2 update course.<br>Complete a new practical assessment. |  |
| More than 2 years                                                                                             | Complete the VHW Stage 1 or 2 training course.<br>Complete a new practical assessment.             |  |
| VHW authorisation status is not valid until an updated authorisation letter is received by the<br>vaccinator. |                                                                                                    |  |

### **A4.11 Other vaccinator authorisation types**

#### A4.11.1 BCG Vaccinators

Only authorised BCG vaccinators can administer the BCG vaccine. All BCG vaccinators are authorised vaccinators with BCG endorsement. For new BCG vaccinators and those seeking regional endorsement see [Table A4.14.](#page-660-0) 

The **[Workforce Requests Portal](https://www.workforcerequests.health.nz/)** will be available for BCG endorsements and renewals through a regional roll out beginning in late 2025. Refer to [Table A4.8.](#page-653-0) for dates when vaccinators start using the portal in each region.

Until the portal is available in your region, your local authorisation requirements and settings will apply as described in [Table A4.15.](#page-661-1)

<span id="page-660-0"></span>**Table A4.14: New BCG vaccinators and gazetted BCG vaccinators seeking regional BCG endorsement**

| Step                                                                 | Requirement                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            |  |  |
|----------------------------------------------------------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--|--|
| To be endorsed as<br>a BCG vaccinator,<br>the applicant needs<br>to: | 1.<br>Complete an approved Vaccinator Foundation Course.<br>2.<br>Be nominated by their employer to become a BCG vaccinator.<br>3.<br>Successfully complete a Health New Zealand   Te Whatu Ora-approved<br>online BCG vaccination course.<br>4.<br>Complete under clinical supervision a minimum of five BCG<br>vaccinations (using a standing order or prescription).<br>5.<br>Successfully complete a BCG clinical assessment by an approved BCG<br>assessor.<br>6.<br>Apply to the Medical Officer of Health for BCG endorsement approval,<br>providing documented evidence of these requirements. |  |  |
| BCG vaccination<br>course                                            | For an initial BCG vaccinator course see IMAC website (available at<br>immune.org.nz, Courses and events).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             |  |  |
| Process if<br>administering<br>additional Schedule<br>vaccines       | Undertake a clinical assessment appropriate for the age group they will be<br>vaccinating. Standing orders or prescriptions are required for the clinical<br>assessment process.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       |  |  |
| Process for two<br>yearly renewal of<br>BCG vaccinator<br>status     | BCG vaccinators must apply to their local Medical Officer of Health prior to<br>the expiry date and provide documented evidence that they:<br>Are a current authorised vaccinator, and<br>•<br>have completed an online vaccination course that includes an update on<br>•<br>BCG.<br>A BCG vaccinator who is an authorised vaccinator will need to complete a<br>standard vaccinator update course, in addition the BCG update.                                                                                                                                                                       |  |  |
| BCG vaccinator<br>update course                                      | Note: BCG vaccinators must complete a BCG Vaccinator Update course every<br>two years. This online course is available as part of IMAC education<br>(available at www.immune.org.nz).                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  |  |  |
| For more<br>information                                              | See the Health New Zealand   Te Whatu Ora webpage BCG vaccine and<br>vaccinator endorsement (available at<br>https://www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/health-services-and<br>programmes/vaccine-information/vaccine-service-delivery/bcg<br>vaccine-and-vaccinator-endorsement                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                   |  |  |

<span id="page-661-1"></span>**Table A4.15: BCG endorsed vaccinator endorsement process using the [Workforce](https://www.workforcerequests.health.nz/)  [Requests Portal](https://www.workforcerequests.health.nz/)**

|      | BCG endorsement for authorised vaccinators                                                                                                                                                                         |  |  |  |  |
|------|--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|--|--|--|--|
| Step |                                                                                                                                                                                                                    |  |  |  |  |
| 1.   | Complete a Health New Zealand   Te Whatu Ora-approved online BCG vaccination course.                                                                                                                               |  |  |  |  |
| 2.   | Apply for endorsement via the Health New Zealand   Te Whatu Ora Workforce Requests Portal.<br>This will enable the vaccinator to administer BCG vaccine, without the need for a standing order or<br>prescription. |  |  |  |  |
| 3.   | Complete supervised practice.                                                                                                                                                                                      |  |  |  |  |
| 5.   | Successfully complete a clinical assessment.                                                                                                                                                                       |  |  |  |  |
| 6.   | Submit clinical assessment documentation to the Health New Zealand   Te Whatu Ora Workforce<br>Requests Portal to be endorsed as either BCG endorsed vaccinator.                                                   |  |  |  |  |

#### A4.11.2 Yellow Fever Vaccinators

Vaccination against yellow fever, exemption from compulsory yellow fever vaccination and provision of approved international certificates of vaccination or prophylaxis, or provision of certificates of medical exemption, are responsibilities devolved by the World Health Organization (WHO) to national health authorities under the International Health Regulations (2005).

Yellow Fever vaccinator authorisation is restricted to registered nurses, nurse practitioners and medical practitioners who must have met strict education requirements and are working at an approved Yellow Fever vaccination centre. Refer to **Yellow Fever – [Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora](https://www.tewhatuora.govt.nz/for-health-professionals/clinical-guidance/diseases-and-conditions/yellow-fever#authorisation-of-yellow-fever-vaccinators-and-vaccination-centres)** and the Yellow Fever Policy for further information.

## <span id="page-661-0"></span>**A4.12 Resuscitation requirements for all vaccinators**

**All** vaccinators, except vaccinating health workers (VHW), need to be able to resuscitate patients and therefore need to achieve and maintain the following resuscitation skills:

- infant, child and adult CPR, and
- one- and two-person bag valve mask ventilation and mouth-to-mask technique,
- administration of IM adrenaline for treatment of anaphylaxis, and
- use of an automated external defibrillator.

Resuscitation training for vaccinators should cover the specific skills outlined above (excluding adrenaline administration, see below). The use of oxygen, sizing of airways, insertion of intravenous lines and the preparation of emergency medications (except for intramuscular adrenaline) are not skills specifically required of a vaccinator.

**All** vaccinators must maintain their current resuscitation certification, typically, this is required by completing an in-person course every two years. (Note: employer protocols may require this more frequently.)

**All** vaccinators, except VHWs, need to be able to administer intramuscular adrenaline in the event of an anaphylactic reaction to an immunisation event (see section [2.3.4\)](#page-87-0). These skills are covered in the Health New Zealand | Te Whatu Ora approved vaccinator courses and are not a requirement of a CPR certification.

VHWs are required to maintain current basic life support skills including:

- carry out infant, child and adult CPR
- must be aware of the signs of anaphylaxis and have the knowledge to treat it but are not expected to administer adrenaline in practice.

**All** vaccinators, including vaccinating health workers (VHW), must meet the emergency equipment and management requirements, regardless of the immunisation setting (eg., in general practice, pharmacies and in non-clinical settings, such as homes, schools, rest homes and workplaces), as listed in sectio[n 2.3.4.](#page-87-0)

**All** vaccinators are expected to keep up to date with any guidance changes, including infection control and requirements for PPE.

# **A4.13 Minimum staff and equipment requirements for vaccination services**

All vaccinators providing immunisation services need to have a minimum of two people present, one of whom must be an authorised vaccinator or a pharmacist vaccinator; the other must be a competent adult who is able to call for emergency support and has a current basic life support certificate.

Vaccinating health workers must work under clinical supervision and direction of a suitably qualified health practitioner at all times.

[Table A4.16](#page-663-0) is a check list for the emergency equipment that is required when vaccinating off-site.

#### <span id="page-663-0"></span>**Table A4.16: Emergency equipment that is required when vaccinating off-site.**

#### **REQUIRED**

- Emergency kit containing:
  - adrenaline 1:1000 (minimum of 3 ampoules) Note EpiPens are not an acceptable alternative as the dose is limited to 300mcg not the required 500mcg for an adult.
  - syringes (1 mL), 25 mm needles for IM injection (minimum of six)
  - adrenaline IM dose chart (ideally laminated)
  - cotton wool balls, gauze
  - Cell phone or phone access
- Sharps box
- Bag valve mask resuscitator (e.g. ambu bag) suitable for the population being vaccinated
- Pocket mask, (recommended specifically where staff have limited skills and/or training with bag valve mask use)

- Cold chain equipment, as required by the National Standards for Vaccine Storage and Transportation for Immunisation Providers 2017 (2nd edition)
- Data logger<sup>a</sup> with a probe, external display and alarm
- Pen and paper for emergency use
- Appropriately sized syringes and needles for specific vaccine programme
- Cotton wool balls, gauze, surgical tape or plasters
- Vomit bowl
- Tissues
- Gloves
- Appropriate surface cleaner
- Approved biohazard bag

#### **OPTIONAL**

- An oxygen cylinder, flow meter, tubing and paediatric/adult masks
- Airways infant through to adult
- Blood pressure monitoring equipment
- Thermometer
- Intravenous cannula and administration sets:
  - Intravenous fluids
  - Hydrocortisone for injection
  - Saline flush
- a. Consider using a secondary back-up device in case the data logger gets damaged. See the *National Standards for Vaccine Storage and Transportation for Immunisation Providers 2017 (2nd edition)* (available at **[https://www.health.govt.nz/publications/national-standards-for-vaccine-storage](https://www.health.govt.nz/publications/national-standards-for-vaccine-storage-and-transportation-for-immunisation-providers-2017)[and-transportation-for-immunisation-providers-2017](https://www.health.govt.nz/publications/national-standards-for-vaccine-storage-and-transportation-for-immunisation-providers-2017)**

# **Appendix 5: Immunisation certificate**

### **A5.1 Introduction**

On 8 January 2026, the Health (Immunisation) Regulations 1995 are being revoked. These regulations required early childhood services and primary schools to maintain onsite immunisation registers by requesting immunisation certificates from parents/guardians of children born from 1 January 1995. These are no longer required as the Aotearoa Immunisation Register (AIR) has replaced this function.

### **A5.2 The Immunisation Certificate**

The Immunisation Certificate will remain available for vaccinators to complete. When completing and signing the immunisation certificate, vaccinators should ensure that a child is fully vaccinated against the AIR record. Children who have not received the necessary doses of a vaccine or have no evidence of laboratory-proven disease should be recorded as 'not fully immunised'.

The immunisation certificate is included in the *Well Child Tamariki Ora My Health Book*. This book also contains the record of the child's vaccinations. Vaccinators should ensure they record all vaccines administered and other relevant health information in this book. This becomes particularly important if the child sees different health professionals. If the child's book is lost, it should be replaced. Copies of the *Well Child Tamariki Ora My Health Book* and immunisation certificate pads can be obtained from the authorised provider of health education materials, usually the local public health service, or ordered from the HealthEd website (**[www.healthed.govt.nz](https://www.healthed.govt.nz/)**).

# **Appendix 6: Passive immunisation**

### **A6.1 Introduction**

Passive immunisation involves administering pre-formed antibody as human immunoglobulin to a recipient who is thought to have either no natural immunity to one or more infections, or who has impaired antibody production. CSL Behring Australia is the primary manufacturer of immunoglobulin products for the New Zealand Blood Service (NZBS). These products are prepared by fractionating large pools of plasma collected from blood donors.

The immunoglobulin products available in New Zealand are usually derived from voluntary, unpaid New Zealand donors who are in good health and who do not have any conditions identifiable by the standard questionnaire that all blood donors complete or by the mandatory testing for HIV/AIDS, hepatitis B, hepatitis C and syphilis on each donation. Blood donations are only used if the tests show no evidence that these infections are present. Similar screening standards apply to the manufacture of other specialist immunoglobulin products which are obtained from an overseas sources. Some of these commercial products may be derived from remunerated, voluntary donors.

### **A6.2 Preparations available in New Zealand**

Immunoglobulin products available in New Zealand include human normal immunoglobulin for intramuscular (IM) use, specific immunoglobulins for intramuscular use, human normal immunoglobulin for intravenous use (IVIG) and human normal immunoglobulin for subcutaneous use (SCIG). All these products have an excellent safety record in both Australia and New Zealand.

#### A6.2.1 Human normal immunoglobulin for intramuscular use

Human normal immunoglobulin for intramuscular is being replaced with an alternative product, GamaSTAN, under Section 29 of the Medicines Act. The production of the previous product, Normal Immunoglobulin-VF, has been discontinued. Any current stocks are due to expire on 24 November 2025. The new product is a sterile, preservative-free solution containing 16.5% protein (mainly human immunoglobulin) made from overseas donations. It is supplied in 10 ml vials (not 5 ml as previously).

NOTE that the dosages of this new product differ from Normal Immunoglobulin-VF. For information about the current human normal immunoglobulins for intramuscular use in New Zealand refer to immunoglobulin preparations section in the **[Transfusion](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/healthcare-professionals/transfusion-medicine/transfusion-medicine-handbook/5-fractionated-products/5-4-immunoglobulin-preparations/)  [Medicine Handbook](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/healthcare-professionals/transfusion-medicine/transfusion-medicine-handbook/5-fractionated-products/5-4-immunoglobulin-preparations/)**.

#### A6.2.2 Specific immunoglobulin for intramuscular use

Specific human immunoglobulin preparations for IM use are available, including those for tetanus, hepatitis B, varicella zoster and anti-D. These are manufactured from plasma pools containing donations from individuals known to have high levels of the appropriate antibody. These preparations are available in single vials containing the specific antibody. The volume of the product will be determined by the potency for the appropriate antibody. In unusual circumstances, when supplies of specific immunoglobulin products manufactured from New Zealand plasma are not available, commercial products from alternative donor sources may be supplied by NZBS.

RIG is imported from a commercial source and is held at NZBS sites in Auckland, Christchurch and Wellington. The product is not registered as a medicine in New Zealand. It may be accessed and supplied under section 29 of the Medicines Act 1981 after discussion with an NZBS medical officer.

#### A6.2.3 Human normal immunoglobulin for intravenous use

For information about the current human normal immunoglobulins for intravenous use in New Zealand refer to immunoglobulin preparations section in the **[Transfusion](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/healthcare-professionals/transfusion-medicine/transfusion-medicine-handbook/5-fractionated-products/5-4-immunoglobulin-preparations/)  [Medicine Handbook](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/healthcare-professionals/transfusion-medicine/transfusion-medicine-handbook/5-fractionated-products/5-4-immunoglobulin-preparations/)**. Further queries regarding usage and dosage of IVIG can be directed to the New Zealand Blood Service transfusion medicine specialists via the district blood bank.

### A6.2.4 Human normal immunoglobulin for subcutaneous use

Human normal immunoglobulin for subcutaneous use (Hizentra or Hizentra NZ) are made from plasma donated by blood donors in Europe and North America or NZBS New Zealand-sourced plasma respectively. Both are sterile solutions containing 20g/100 mL of human plasma protein with a purity of at least 98 percent immunoglobulin G (IgG). The distribution of the IgG subclasses closely resembles that found in normal human plasma. The manufacturing process involves special steps to reduce the possibility of virus transmission, including pH 4 incubation and nanofiltration. For further details and information on product in use refer to: **[https://www.nzblood.co.nz/healthcare-professionals/immunoglobulin-ig](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/healthcare-professionals/immunoglobulin-ig-hub/subcutaneous-immunoglobulins-scig/hizentra/)[hub/subcutaneous-immunoglobulins-scig/hizentra/](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/healthcare-professionals/immunoglobulin-ig-hub/subcutaneous-immunoglobulins-scig/hizentra/)**

#### A6.2.5 Accessing immunoglobulin or contacting NZBS for advice

NZBS operates a 24-hour on-call service for medical advice and access to these products. Details of the medical officer on call can be obtained from any district hospital blood bank in New Zealand.

Product can be requested using the NZBS request form. This can be accessed online (**[www.nzblood.co.nz/Clinical-information/Transfusion-medicine/Information-for-](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/Clinical-information/Transfusion-medicine/Information-for-Health-Professionals/Request-forms)[Health-Professionals/Request-forms](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/Clinical-information/Transfusion-medicine/Information-for-Health-Professionals/Request-forms)**), by contacting your local blood bank or writing to:

Private Bag 92071 Victoria Street West Auckland 1142 email: **[info@nzblood.co.nz](mailto:info@nzblood.co.nz)**

telephone (during normal office hours): 09 523 5744.

### **A6.3 Indications for use**

#### A6.3.1 Passive immunisation

New Zealand Blood Service

For advice on the use of immunoglobulin products and specific dosages of these products, please contact a medical officer at NZBS. Copies of the product data sheet are available on the NZBS website (**[www.nzblood.co.nz/Clinical](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/Clinical-information/Transfusion-medicine/Health-professionals-medicine-datasheets/Immunoglobulins)[information/Transfusion-medicine/Health-professionals-medicine](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/Clinical-information/Transfusion-medicine/Health-professionals-medicine-datasheets/Immunoglobulins)[datasheets/Immunoglobulins](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/Clinical-information/Transfusion-medicine/Health-professionals-medicine-datasheets/Immunoglobulins)** ).

Normal Immunoglobulin-VF is available for passive immunisation (pre- or postexposure prophylaxis) against measles (see section [12.8.1\)](#page-356-0) and hepatitis A (see section [8.8\)](#page-249-0) where active vaccination is not appropriate or is contraindicated. It is not recommended for the prevention of rubella or mumps. Guidance on the use of specific preparations is provided in other sections of this *Handbook*: for pre- or post-exposure prophylaxis against hepatitis B (section [9.5.2](#page-265-0) and section [9.8.1\)](#page-276-0), tetanus (section [22.5.5\)](#page-532-0) and varicella zoster (section [24.8.2\)](#page-571-0).

#### A6.3.2 Management of primary and acquired immune deficiency

Recurrent infections can occur in individuals who have low or absent levels of circulating immunoglobulins – so-called humoral immune deficiency. This can arise as a congenital disorder, or it can be acquired as a consequence of a number of diseases. Humoral immune deficiency can exist alone or as part of a wider immune deficiency syndrome. Immunoglobulin products can be used to prevent recurrent infections in these patients.

Until recently, IVIG was the product of choice for managing these patients. A subcutaneous IgG product (Evogam) is also now available, which can be infused by patients at home. This avoids the need for outpatient or day-case admission for infusion of IVIG and is preferred by some patients. The subcutaneous preparation is not suitable for use in prophylaxis against hepatitis A or measles infection.

For replacement therapy in antibody deficiency disorders, monthly administration of IVIG is given, usually at a dosage of 0.2 to 0.6 g/kg of body weigh[t.](#page-673-0)<sup>1</sup> Subcutaneous product is administered one to two times per week, with the overall monthly dosage similar to that of IVIG. For both types of product, the dosage and frequency of infusion should be based on the effectiveness in the individual patient. In general, however, the aim of treatment should be to maintain the serum IgG at or above a level of 5 g/L.

### **A6.4 Storage and administration**

Immunoglobulin products must be stored at +2°C to +8°C and must not be frozen. They should also be protected from light. If the product appears turbid or contains sediment, it must not be used. Always check and observe the manufacturer's expiry date before injecting the product. The product does not contain an antimicrobial preservative and must be used immediately after opening the vial; any unused portions should be discarded. Information on the batch number and dose injected must be kept in the recipient's records.

The intramuscular and subcutaneous forms of normal immunoglobulin should be brought to room temperature before use. They *must not* be given intravenously because of the possible reactions discussed in section [A6.7.](#page-672-0) [1](#page-673-0)

The intramuscular product, Normal Immunoglobulin-VF, should be given slowly by deep IM injection, using a needle of appropriate gauge and length. If a large volume (more than 5 mL) is required, administration in divided doses at different sites is recommended.

The subcutaneous product, Evogam, is normally given using an infusion pump. Information on infusion rates is provided in the medicine's data sheet.

### A6.4.1 Interactions with other drugs

Immunoglobulin should not be mixed with other pharmaceutical products, except as indicated by the manufacturer.

**Passively acquired antibody can interfere with the response to live attenuated virus vaccines.** Live virus vaccines should be given at least two weeks before, or deferred for up to 11 months after, doses of human normal immunoglobulin or other blood product[s.](#page-673-1)<sup>2</sup> The interval will be determined by the blood product and dose received (see [Table A6.1\)](#page-669-0).

[Table A6.1](#page-669-0) gives the current recommendations for deferment of live parenteral vaccines, MMR and VV, after receipt of blood products. Note that the live vaccines will not cause harm if given earlier than this, but the immune response may be suboptimal, leaving a patient at risk for the relevant disease(s). In an outbreak situation (e.g. measles) immunisation may be given at any time as the benefit could outweigh the potential impaired response. If given earlier, repeating live vaccine dose (or subsequent serological testing) at an appropriate time interval can be done.

If MMR (dose 1 and/or dose 2) or VV are administered earlier than the recommended stand-down period following receipt of immunoglobulin and/or blood products, to

maintain appropriately aligned childhood Schedule and protection from measles or varicella, a further dose (ie MMR3) is recommended. An additional dose of MMR or VV should be given at the appropriate interval after the immunoglobulin and/or blood product to ensure adequate immunity.

#### <span id="page-669-0"></span>**Table A6.1: Suggested intervals between immunoglobulin and blood product administration or blood transfusion and MMR or varicella vaccination**

Note that these live vaccines will not cause harm if given earlier than these intervals, but the immune response could be suboptimal, leaving a patient at risk for the relevant disease(s). To maintain an appropriately aligned childhood Schedule or in an outbreak situation (e.g. measles) immunisation may be given at any time as the benefit could outweigh the potential impaired response. If MMR or VV is administered earlier than the recommended interval, an additional dose MMR or VV to be given at the appropriate interval after the receipt of immunoglobulin and/or blood product to ensure adequate immunity.

#### Notes

- a. Unvaccinated persons might not be protected fully against measles during the entire recommended interval, and additional doses of immunoglobulin or measles vaccine might be indicated after measles exposure.
- b. IMPORTANT check product if before 24 November 2025. Dosages given are for GamaSTAN (see **[Transfusion medicine handbook](https://www.nzblood.co.nz/healthcare-professionals/transfusion-medicine/transfusion-medicine-handbook/5-fractionated-products/5-4-immunoglobulin-preparations)** )
- c. Immunoglobulin is not normally available or recommended in New Zealand for pre-travel use.
- d. Cytomegalovirus immunoglobulin is not available in New Zealand. Contact NZBS MO to discuss access to an alternative product.
- e. Palivizumab contains antibody only to respiratory syncytial virus and does not interfere with the immune response to live or inactivated vaccines. For further information, see Starship guidelines **[starship.org.nz/guidelines/palivizumab-synagis-r-immunisation-for-respiratory-syncytial-virus](https://starship.org.nz/guidelines/palivizumab-synagis-r-immunisation-for-respiratory-syncytial-virus-rsv/)[rsv](https://starship.org.nz/guidelines/palivizumab-synagis-r-immunisation-for-respiratory-syncytial-virus-rsv/)**
- f. Other fractionated blood products may contain traces of immunoglobulin. Contact a transfusion medicine specialist to discuss potential impact on vaccination.

Adapted from: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention. 2011. General recommendations on immunization. Recommendations of the Advisory Committee on Immunization Practices (ACIP). *Morbidity and Mortality Weekly Report* 60(RR2): 1–61. Table 5. Deferral interval for vaccination after blood components and products calculated from NZBS data.

Note: These intervals do not apply to BCG vaccines and rotavirus vaccines in infants, nor to zoster vaccine for individuals 50 years of age, the efficacy of which is unaffected by the presence of passive immunoglobulin.

Inactivated vaccines may be administered concurrently with passive antibody (although in separate syringes) to induce active immunity, as is done for some tetanus-prone wounds and for babies born to HBsAg-positive mothers.

#### A6.4.2 Passive transfer of antibodies and interference with serological testing

Serological testing after the administration of immunoglobulin may detect transfused antibodies for several months after administration. Serological testing for any infection after immunoglobulin should therefore be discussed with an expert.

### **A6.5 Duration of effect**

- The estimated half-life of **intramuscular** human normal immunoglobulin is 27 ± 7 days (mean ± standard deviation [SD]).[1](#page-673-0) The duration of effect is linked to the initial dosage.
- The estimated half-life of **intravenous** human normal immunoglobulin is 40 ± 8 days (mean ± SD[\).](#page-673-0)<sup>1</sup>
- The estimated half-life of **subcutaneous** human normal immunoglobulin is 55 days (range 14–165 days).[1](#page-673-0)

### **A6.6 Contraindications and precautions**

#### A6.6.1 Contraindications

Immunoglobulin products intended for subcutaneous and intramuscular injection must not be administered intravenously because of the potential for anaphylactic reactions.

Health professionals should check the package insert for the immunoglobulin product to be administered.

Skin tests should not be conducted with immunoglobulin preparations. Intradermal injection of any concentrated immunoglobulin product may cause a local inflammatory reaction, which can be misinterpreted as a positive allergic reaction. Allergic responses to normal immunoglobulin given in the prescribed IM route are extremely rare, but may occur in those with complete immunoglobulin A (IgA) deficiency in whom anti-IgA is present.

Intramuscular injection of immunoglobulin products should be avoided in patients with a low platelet count or with any coagulation disorder that would contraindicate IM injections. In these circumstances, the injection may be given subcutaneously, with a lightly applied pressure pad if prone to bruising; for example, if thrombocytopenia or von Willebrand disease is present.[3](#page-673-2)

#### A6.6.2 Precautions

Injections of Normal Immunoglobulin-VF must be IM, and care should be taken to draw back on the plunger of the syringe before injection to be certain that the needle is not in a blood vessel (see section [2.2.3\)](#page-73-0).

As with any injection, there is a risk of anaphylaxis. Adrenaline and other means of treating acute reactions should therefore be immediately available (see section [2.3.4\)](#page-87-0).

## <span id="page-672-0"></span>**A6.7 Potential responses and adverse events following passive immunisation**

Clinicians in New Zealand are requested to notify all adverse reactions arising from, or in association with, the use of blood products. Reactions to any immunoglobulin product should be reported on a form obtainable from NZBS or any local district hospital blood bank.

Local tenderness, erythema and muscle stiffness occasionally occur at the site of injection and may persist for several hours after intramuscular injection. An occasional recipient may react more strongly, with a low-grade fever. Systemic reactions, including nausea, urticaria and generalised hypersensitivity reactions, may occur.[1,](#page-673-0) [3](#page-673-2)

Reactions to IVIG tend to be related to the infusion rate and are most likely to occur during the first hour of the infusion. However, delayed reactions can occur, and include nausea, vomiting, chest pains, rigors, dizziness and aching legs. Systemic and local reactions are more common in those being treated for hypogammaglobulinaemia than in those with normal gammaglobulin levels who are being treated with immunoglobulin preparations for autoimmune conditions.

Occasional reports exist of renal failure following infusion of IVIG. These largely relate to sucrose-containing products. The IVIG products available in New Zealand, do not contain sucrose, but patients should be adequately hydrated prior to their administration. Renal function should be monitored in patients considered to be at increased risk.

Aseptic meningitis has been reported following treatment with IVIG. This may present up to two days following treatment. Anaphylactic reactions, although rare, have been reported following injection of immunoglobulin products, although anaphylaxis is more likely to occur following intravenous infusion. Other significant adverse events that have been observed in New Zealand and are mostly associated with large or ongoing treatment with high-dose IVIG or SCIG include: haemolysis, rashes, febrile events, pain or hypotension.

Immunoglobulin products may interfere with the immune response to live virus vaccines. In general, live vaccines should be given at least two weeks before or up to 11 months after the immunoglobulin preparation (see [Table A6.1\)](#page-669-0). This does not apply to the yellow fever vaccine, because New Zealand blood donors are very unlikely to

have antibodies to this virus. For travellers abroad, the necessary interval may not be possible. No evidence of adverse interaction with rotavirus vaccine has been reported.

See section [1.6.3](#page-47-0) for further information about adverse events and reporting.
